stem: A hospital uses an ELISA screening test to screen for Lyme disease. The test has a sensitivity of 88% and a specificity of 81%. The prevalence of Lyme disease in the patient population is estimated to be between 9.6% and 21.5%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has Lyme disease? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(Lyme disease | positive test) ranges from 0.33 to 0.559 as prevalence varies from 9.6% to 21.5%. Point estimate at 15.5% prevalence: 0.459.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.19.
The confidence interval [0.33, 0.559] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (88%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
